summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's sweeping proposal for DMA compliance in the EU, arguing that Apple has outmaneuvered the European Commission by creating a system so complex that the path of least resistance for developers remains the status quo. The Core Technology Fee — €0.50 per install above 1 million — is the centerpiece, effectively a poison pill for companies like Meta, Spotify, and Google that currently pay Apple nothing for free app distribution. Apple's proposal only permits sideloading through regulated third-party marketplaces (not direct web downloads), still requires notarization of all apps, and makes opting into new EU terms irrevocable. Gruber frames the conflict as two equally proud, stubborn institutions colliding, but bets Apple is the smarter player. He notes the delicious irony that Apple now faces the same uncertainty with EU regulators that developers have always faced with App Store review.
key_points: Apple's DMA compliance creates a deliberately complex system where staying on existing App Store terms is the simplest option, preserving the status quo for most developers
The Core Technology Fee targets free apps from Apple's biggest rivals — Meta, Google, Spotify — who currently pay Apple nothing, potentially costing them hundreds of millions annually
Third-party app distribution is only permitted through regulated marketplace apps, not direct web downloads, and Apple still reviews all apps for technical (but not content) compliance
The DMA is characterized as anti-U.S.-big-business rather than pro-consumer, with aspects seemingly written specifically for Spotify's benefit
Apple's proposals are just that — proposals — and the EC may reject them, creating an ironic parallel to developers' own experience with App Store review
money_quotes: Apple is proud, stubborn, arrogant, controlling, and convinced it has the best interests of its customers in mind. The European Commission is proud, stubborn, arrogant, controlling, and convinced it has the best interests of its citizens in mind.
I'd go slightly further and describe the DMA as anti-U.S.-big-business, because as far as I can tell, nothing in the DMA adversely affects or even annoys any European tech companies.
The European Commission's priorities put developers first, users second, and 'gatekeepers' a distant third. The DMA prescribes not a win-win-win framework, but a win-win-lose one.

Ever since this collision over the DMA seemed inevitable, starting about two years ago, I've been trying to imagine how it would turn out. And each time, I start by asking: Which side is smarter? My money has been on Apple.
The EC's obsession with payment processing and commissions blinded them, I think, to the fact that Apple has always had other options for monetization.

The delicious irony in Apple's not knowing if these massive, complicated proposals will be deemed DMA-compliant is that their dealings with the European Commission sound exactly like App Store developers' dealings with Apple.
That the DMA declares Apple's existing terms no longer acceptable does not mean Apple's only compliant response would be to cede most control over iOS.
Consider simply that Google pays Apple $20-fucking-BILLION dollars per year to keep Google Search as the default search engine in Safari.
tone: analytical, opinionated, admiring of Apple's strategy
key_insight: Apple's DMA compliance proposal is a masterclass in malicious compliance — technically meeting EU requirements while engineering a system so complex and costly that the rational choice for most developers remains the status quo Apple always preferred.
